labor__delivery_history.other_interventions_or_complications: Labor was induced at 39 weeks due to maternal gestational hypertension that had been monitored for about 3 weeks prior. Induction started with oral misoprostol for cervical ripening, followed by Pitocin (oxytocin) drip the next morning. Fetal heart rate tracing showed a brief period of variable decelerations during active labor, which resolved with position changes (side-lying, then hands-and-knees) and temporarily reducing the Pitocin rate. No intrauterine pressure catheter or fetal scalp electrode was needed. There was a second-degree perineal tear that was repaired with dissolvable sutures immediately after delivery. Estimated blood loss was within normal range, and no postpartum hemorrhage occurred. Baby cried immediately after birth and required only routine suctioning and stimulation, no NICU admission.
labor__delivery_history.other_concerns_or_notable_remarks_about_: This was the mother’s first pregnancy and delivery. Total labor time from the start of regular contractions after Pitocin to birth was about 11 hours. The mother experienced significant back labor, which improved somewhat after the epidural was placed. Pushing lasted approximately 45 minutes. The baby had a loose nuchal cord (umbilical cord around the neck once), which the obstetrician was able to slip over the head without cutting it, and there were no signs of distress afterward. Delayed cord clamping was done for about 90 seconds. Skin-to-skin contact was initiated within a few minutes of birth and continued for the majority of the first hour, which helped with early breastfeeding attempts. The mother reported feeling very supported by the nursing staff and her partner, though she did experience shaking and nausea shortly after the epidural placement, which resolved without medication. Overall, both mother and baby recovered well, and they were discharged home 36 hours after delivery with no additional complications.

growth__development_history.if_yes_how_long_breastfeeding: Exclusively breastfed for approximately 5 months, then breast milk plus some formula from 5–8 months, and continued partial breastfeeding (mostly morning and bedtime feeds) until about 13 months of age.
growth__development_history.if_yes_at_what_age_formula: Around 5 months old, we began supplementing with formula for one bottle a day when the mother returned to part-time work.
growth__development_history.if_yes_what_type_formula: Initially used Similac Pro-Advance (cow’s milk–based formula) and later switched to Enfamil NeuroPro Sensitive around 7 months due to mild gassiness and fussiness, which improved after the switch.
growth__development_history.if_yes_please_explain_colic_reflux_const: From about 3 weeks to roughly 10–11 weeks of age, he had classic evening colic: intense crying spells starting around 6–7 PM and sometimes lasting 1–2 hours, during which he would pull his legs up, arch his back, and seem very uncomfortable. The pediatrician evaluated him and felt it was benign colic and possibly mild reflux. He spit up small amounts frequently but was still gaining weight well, so no medication was started. We tried keeping him upright for at least 20–30 minutes after feeds, used paced feeding when we introduced the occasional bottle, and did frequent burping during feeds. We also tried a probiotic drop (Lactobacillus reuteri) for about a month, which may have helped a bit. Constipation was only a minor issue when he first started solids (around 5.5–6 months); stools became firmer and less frequent. We added more pureed prunes, pears, and a little extra water, and that helped normalize his bowel movements without needing medication. There were no episodes of blood in the stool or concerning vomiting, and symptoms gradually resolved by about 3 months for the colic and by 7–8 months for the refluxy spit-up.
growth__development_history.age_child_responded_to_sound: We first noticed him clearly startle and then turn his head toward loud sounds (like the dog barking or the door closing) at around 2–3 weeks old. By about 1 month, he consistently turned his head toward our voices when we spoke from one side.
growth__development_history.age_child_followed_an_object: Around 6 weeks old he was reliably tracking a brightly colored rattle or our faces from side to side. By about 2 months, he could follow an object in a wider arc and would visually track toys across his midline.
growth__development_history.age_child_held_their_head_up: He could momentarily lift his head during tummy time at around 4 weeks, but by about 2.5 months he could hold his head up steadily while on his tummy and when held upright against our chest. By 3 months, head control was strong and consistent.
growth__development_history.age_child_vocalized: He began cooing and making more purposeful vowel sounds (like “ahh” and “ooh”) around 6–8 weeks. By 4–5 months he was babbling with consonant-vowel combinations such as “ba,” “da,” and “ma,” and would respond to us talking with long strings of babble, especially during diaper changes and playtime.
growth__development_history.age_child_teethed: His first two bottom front teeth (lower central incisors) came in just before 7 months of age. He had increased drooling and chewing on his hands and toys starting around 5 months. By around 12 months he had eight teeth (four on top, four on bottom).
growth__development_history.age_child_sat_alone: He could sit with minimal support by about 5.5 months, using his hands to prop himself up. By around 6.5 months, he was able to sit independently without using his hands for several minutes at a time and could reach for toys without toppling over.
growth__development_history.age_child_crawled: He began army crawling (dragging himself forward on his belly) at about 7 months, then transitioned to a more coordinated hands-and-knees crawl around 8 months. By 9 months he was crawling quickly and efficiently, exploring the whole living room and following us from room to room.
growth__development_history.age_child_walked: He started pulling to stand and cruising along furniture at around 10–11 months. He took his first independent steps just after his first birthday (about 12.5 months) and was walking more confidently and steadily by 13–14 months.
growth__development_history.age_child_began_cows_milk: We introduced whole cow’s milk in a cup around 12 months as we began to wean daytime breastfeeding. By about 14 months, he was primarily drinking whole cow’s milk and water, with no formula and only occasional comfort nursing at night.
growth__development_history.age_child_began_solid_foods: We started offering simple purees (single-ingredient foods like sweet potato, avocado, banana, and oatmeal) at about 5.5–6 months, once he could sit with support and showed interest in watching us eat. We gradually increased the variety and texture of his foods over the next few months, including soft finger foods around 7–8 months (small pieces of steamed vegetables, soft fruits, scrambled eggs, and well-cooked pasta).
growth__development_history.food_intolerance_or_allergies_and_when_t: No diagnosed food allergies. At around 9 months, we noticed he sometimes developed a mild perioral rash (small red bumps around the mouth) after eating tomato-based sauces and certain acidic fruits like strawberries. The rash always resolved on its own within a couple of hours and was not associated with any swelling, breathing difficulty, or vomiting. The pediatrician felt this was more of a contact irritation than a true allergy. We still offer those foods in moderation and apply a thin layer of barrier cream around his mouth beforehand, which seems to help. No issues with peanut, egg, dairy, wheat, soy, or fish so far.
growth__development_history.childs_hospitalization_and_surgical_hist: He has not had any hospital admissions or surgeries. Only routine outpatient pediatric visits and one urgent care visit in 2024 for a persistent cough and fever, which turned out to be a viral upper respiratory infection. No ER visits or overnight stays.
growth__development_history.major_injuries_accidents_falls_and_or_fr: In early 2025, when he was about 18 months old, he slipped while running on the playground and fell forward, hitting his forehead on a rubber mat. He had a medium-sized goose egg (forehead bump) but did not lose consciousness or vomit. We took him to urgent care; they performed a neurological exam, which was normal, and determined that imaging was not necessary. We were given head injury precautions and monitored him closely at home. The swelling and bruising resolved over about a week, and there have been no lingering issues. No fractures or serious injuries to date.
growth__development_history.vaccination_reactions: He has received all recommended routine vaccinations on schedule (including Hep B, DTaP, IPV, Hib, PCV13, Rotavirus, MMR, Varicella, Hep A, and annual influenza). The only reactions have been mild and self-limited: low-grade fevers (up to about 100.5°F) for 24 hours after some of the 2-, 4-, and 6-month shots, and a small, tender lump with redness at the injection site on his thigh that resolved within 2–3 days. We usually give infant acetaminophen for comfort if he seems fussy or uncomfortable. No history of severe allergic reactions, difficulty breathing, widespread hives, or hospital visits related to vaccines.
growth__development_history.if_yes_how_many_times_and_list_reason_an:
He has received antibiotics twice so far:
1) At 14 months (2024): Amoxicillin for 10 days for a right-sided acute otitis media (ear infection). Symptoms included fever, ear tugging, and irritability. He responded well to treatment and symptoms resolved within a few days.
2) At 20 months (2025): Amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin) for 10 days for a sinus infection following a prolonged cold with thick nasal discharge and nighttime coughing. Again, he improved significantly after starting antibiotics with no side effects other than slightly looser stools toward the end of the course.
growth__development_history.if_yes_please_explain_night_terrors_diff: He has generally been a decent sleeper, but starting around 16–17 months we noticed occasional episodes that seemed like night terrors. About 1–2 times per month, usually within the first 2–3 hours after falling asleep, he will suddenly sit up in his crib crying intensely, sometimes screaming and thrashing, with his eyes open but glassy and not really focused. When we try to comfort him, he often doesn’t seem to recognize us, and attempts to pick him up sometimes make him more agitated. These episodes last about 5–10 minutes and then he settles back down on his own and goes back to sleep, often without fully waking up or remembering anything the next morning. We brought this up with his pediatrician, who agreed they were likely night terrors related to normal sleep development and not seizures. They recommended keeping a consistent bedtime routine, avoiding overtiredness, and ensuring a calm, dim environment before bed. Since we started putting him down a bit earlier and limiting stimulating activities (no screens, quieter play in the hour before bedtime), the episodes have become slightly less frequent and less intense. On regular nights, he sleeps roughly from 7:30 PM to 6:30 AM with one brief awakening for water or a quick cuddle. He still takes one nap in the early afternoon, typically 1–2 hours.
